Assignment 5

Lucian Scher

2026-05-19

This assignment works from Lab 5. Setup below is copied from lab 5. Assignment uses a stratified training sample for decision-tree CV/tuning so tune_grid() runs faster.

Setup

This full data set includes more possible predictors than the text alone, but for this model we will only use the text variable

urlfile ="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MaRo406/EDS-231-text-sentiment/main/data/climbing_reports_model_dat.csv"
incidents_df<-readr::read_csv(url(urlfile))
# How many incidents are marked as deadly?

Now we’ll split our data into training and test portions

set.seed(321)
incidents2class <- incidents_df |> 
  mutate(deadly = factor(ifelse(
    is.na(Deadly),
    "non-deadly", "deadly"
  )))

#split our data
incidents_split <- initial_split(incidents2class, prop = 0.8, strata = deadly)

incidents_train <- training(incidents_split)
incidents_test <- testing(incidents_split)

We use recipe() to specify the predictor and outcome variables and the data.

incidents_rec <- recipe(deadly ~ Text, data = incidents_train)

Next we add some familiar pre-processing steps on our Text variable: tokenize to word level, filter to the most common words, and calculate tf-idf.

recipe <- incidents_rec %>%
  step_tokenize(Text) %>%
  step_tokenfilter(Text, max_tokens = 1000) %>%
  step_tfidf(Text)  # lab: step_tfidf(); name Text for textrecipes >= 1.1

Create a tidymodels workflow to combine the modeling components

incidents_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(recipe)
nb_spec <- naive_Bayes() %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>% #set modeling context
  set_engine("naivebayes") #method for fitting model

Time for model evaluation. We’ll do a standard resampling process to evaluate our naive Bayes model. Here we create 10-fold cross-validation sets, and use them to estimate performance. vfold_cv let’s us do this.

set.seed(123)
incidents_folds <- vfold_cv(incidents_train)
nb_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(recipe) %>%
  add_model(nb_spec)

To estimate its performance, we fit the model to each of the resampled folds, and then evaluate on the heldout part of each resampled fold.

nb_rs <- tune::fit_resamples(
  nb_wf,
  incidents_folds,
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

Extract the relevant information using collect_metrics() and collect_predictions() and examine the performance metrics.

nb_rs_metrics <- collect_metrics(nb_rs)
nb_rs_predictions <- collect_predictions(nb_rs)
nb_rs_metrics 
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   .metric     .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
##   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy    binary     0.802    10 0.00986 pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 brier_class binary     0.198    10 0.00987 pre0_mod0_post0
## 3 roc_auc     binary     0.733    10 0.0114  pre0_mod0_post0

We’ll use two performance metrics: accuracy and ROC AUC. Accuracy is the proportion of the data that is predicted correctly. The ROC curve plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate; AUC closer to 1 indicates a better-performing model, while AUC closer to 0.5 indicates a model that does no better than random guessing.

nb_rs_predictions %>%
  group_by(id) %>%
  roc_curve(truth = deadly, .pred_deadly) %>%
  autoplot() +
  labs(
    color = "Resamples",
    title = "ROC curve for Climbing Incident Reports"
  )

Another model method involves the confusion matrix. A confusion matrix tabulates a model’s false positives and false negatives for each class.

conf_mat_resampled(nb_rs, tidy = FALSE) %>% #compute matrix for each fold then average
  autoplot(type = "heatmap")

Hmm, let’s try a different model.

Lasso classification uses regularization on regression to produce a simpler, more generalizable model. It learns how much of a penalty to apply to features, reducing the high-dimensional space of tokens — sometimes shrinking coefficients all the way to 0, effectively removing those features from the model entirely.

The penalty is a hyperparameter we need to choose. Rather than setting it manually, we estimate its best value by fitting models across 30 candidate penalty values on our resampled data and comparing performance.

tune_spec <- logistic_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 1) %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("glmnet")

tune_spec
## Logistic Regression Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   penalty = tune()
##   mixture = 1
## 
## Computational engine: glmnet
# mixture = 1 is fixed in tune_spec (lasso); grid only varies penalty
lambda_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(), levels = 30)

lambda_grid
## # A tibble: 30 × 1
##     penalty
##       <dbl>
##  1 1   e-10
##  2 2.21e-10
##  3 4.89e-10
##  4 1.08e- 9
##  5 2.40e- 9
##  6 5.30e- 9
##  7 1.17e- 8
##  8 2.59e- 8
##  9 5.74e- 8
## 10 1.27e- 7
## # ℹ 20 more rows

Here we use grid_regular() to create 30 possible values for the regularization penalty. Then tune_grid() fits a model at each of those values.

tune_wf <- workflow() %>% 
  add_recipe(recipe) %>% 
  add_model(tune_spec)

set.seed(2023)
tune_rs <- tune_grid(
  tune_wf,
  incidents_folds,
  grid = lambda_grid,
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = T)
  
)
collect_metrics(tune_rs)
## # A tibble: 90 × 7
##     penalty .metric     .estimator  mean     n std_err .config         
##       <dbl> <chr>       <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>           
##  1 1   e-10 accuracy    binary     0.886    10 0.00502 pre0_mod01_post0
##  2 1   e-10 brier_class binary     0.102    10 0.00364 pre0_mod01_post0
##  3 1   e-10 roc_auc     binary     0.919    10 0.00554 pre0_mod01_post0
##  4 2.21e-10 accuracy    binary     0.886    10 0.00502 pre0_mod02_post0
##  5 2.21e-10 brier_class binary     0.102    10 0.00364 pre0_mod02_post0
##  6 2.21e-10 roc_auc     binary     0.919    10 0.00554 pre0_mod02_post0
##  7 4.89e-10 accuracy    binary     0.886    10 0.00502 pre0_mod03_post0
##  8 4.89e-10 brier_class binary     0.102    10 0.00364 pre0_mod03_post0
##  9 4.89e-10 roc_auc     binary     0.919    10 0.00554 pre0_mod03_post0
## 10 1.08e- 9 accuracy    binary     0.886    10 0.00502 pre0_mod04_post0
## # ℹ 80 more rows
autoplot(tune_rs) + 
  labs(
    title = "Lasso performance across regularization penalties"
  )

tune_rs %>%
  show_best(metric = "roc_auc")
## # A tibble: 5 × 7
##    penalty .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config         
##      <dbl> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>           
## 1 0.00386  roc_auc binary     0.945    10 0.00489 pre0_mod23_post0
## 2 0.00853  roc_auc binary     0.943    10 0.00498 pre0_mod24_post0
## 3 0.00174  roc_auc binary     0.941    10 0.00491 pre0_mod22_post0
## 4 0.000788 roc_auc binary     0.935    10 0.00482 pre0_mod21_post0
## 5 0.0189   roc_auc binary     0.933    10 0.00609 pre0_mod25_post0
tune_rs %>%
  show_best(metric = "accuracy")
## # A tibble: 5 × 7
##    penalty .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config         
##      <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>           
## 1 0.00386  accuracy binary     0.909    10 0.00471 pre0_mod23_post0
## 2 0.00174  accuracy binary     0.906    10 0.00492 pre0_mod22_post0
## 3 0.000788 accuracy binary     0.901    10 0.00492 pre0_mod21_post0
## 4 0.00853  accuracy binary     0.900    10 0.00557 pre0_mod24_post0
## 5 0.000356 accuracy binary     0.894    10 0.00566 pre0_mod20_post0
chosen_acc <- tune_rs %>% 
  select_by_one_std_err(metric = "accuracy", -penalty)

Next, let’s finalize our workflow with this particular regularization penalty. This is the regularization penalty that our tuning results indicate give us the best model.

final_lasso <- tune_wf %>%
  finalize_workflow(chosen_acc)

final_lasso
## ══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## Preprocessor: Recipe
## Model: logistic_reg()
## 
## ── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## 3 Recipe Steps
## 
## • step_tokenize()
## • step_tokenfilter()
## • step_tfidf()
## 
## ── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Logistic Regression Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   penalty = 0.00385662042116347
##   mixture = 1
## 
## Computational engine: glmnet

The penalty argument value now reflects our tuning result. Now we fit to our training data.

fitted_lasso <- fit(final_lasso, incidents_train)

First let’s look at the words associated with an accident being non-deadly.

# fitted_lasso

And now the words that are most associated with a deadly incident.

# fitted_lasso

Finally, let’s fit to the test data and see how we did.

last_fit(final_lasso, incidents_split) %>%
  collect_metrics()
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##   .metric     .estimator .estimate .config        
##   <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy    binary        0.919  pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 roc_auc     binary        0.951  pre0_mod0_post0
## 3 brier_class binary        0.0620 pre0_mod0_post0

Assignment

Third classifier: Decision tree using rpart.

CV and hyperparameter tuning below use a 25% stratified sample of incidents_train and 5-fold CV so tune_grid() finishes in reasonable time. Task 3–4 last_fit() uses the full incidents_split.

library(rpart)
# Stratified sample for faster decision-tree CV/tuning only (full data used in last_fit)
set.seed(99)
incidents_train_sample <- incidents_train |>
  group_by(deadly) |>
  slice_sample(prop = 0.25) |>
  ungroup()

set.seed(123)
incidents_folds_sample <- vfold_cv(incidents_train_sample, v = 5)

Task 1 — Cross-validated performance (default decision tree)

Claude helped me set up the default decision tree specification and workflow below. I asked it to use the rpart engine because it is a common implementation of decision trees in R.

# build default decision tree  
dt_spec_default <- decision_tree() %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("rpart")
# Same as lab workflow but with the default decision tree spec
dt_wf_default <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(recipe) %>%
  add_model(dt_spec_default)
# 5-fold CV on 25% sample
dt_rs_default <- fit_resamples(
  dt_wf_default,
  incidents_folds_sample,
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)
# Mean accuracy and ROC AUC across resamples
dt_cv_metrics <- collect_metrics(dt_rs_default)

print(dt_cv_metrics)
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   .metric     .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
##   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy    binary     0.881     5  0.0174 pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 brier_class binary     0.107     5  0.0131 pre0_mod0_post0
## 3 roc_auc     binary     0.837     5  0.0254 pre0_mod0_post0

Task 1 — Summary

Our cross-validated accuracy is 0.881 and roc_auc is 0.837. Which is better than the naive Bayes CV performance. But our decision tree CV is on a smaller sample and fewer folds, so this is just a rough baseline comparison.

Task 2 — Hyperparameter tuning

# Mark cost_complexity and tree_depth as tunable
dt_spec_tune <- decision_tree(
  cost_complexity = tune(),
  tree_depth = tune()
) %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("rpart")
# Build 5 x 5 grid of cost_complexity and tree_depth values to try
dt_param_grid <- grid_regular(
  cost_complexity(),
  tree_depth(),
  levels = 5
)

Claude suggested tuning these two hyperparameters because they control the complexity of the decision tree. I asked it to create a grid of 5 x 5 = 25 combinations of these hyperparameters to try during tuning.

# Fit each grid point with 5-fold CV on the sample
dt_wf_tune <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(recipe) %>%
  add_model(dt_spec_tune)

set.seed(456)
dt_tune_rs <- tune_grid(
  dt_wf_tune,
  incidents_folds_sample,
  grid = dt_param_grid,
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)
# Visualize how accuracy / ROC AUC change across the grid
autoplot(dt_tune_rs) +
  labs(title = "Decision tree CV performance across hyperparameters (sample)")

Claude helped me figure out the best hyperparameter values based on the tuning results. I asked it to use the “one standard error rule” to select a simpler tree that is within 1 std error of the best ROC AUC, to help reduce overfitting.

# Top-performing penalty/depth combinations on the sample
dt_tune_rs %>% show_best(metric = "roc_auc")
## # A tibble: 5 × 8
##   cost_complexity tree_depth .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config     
##             <dbl>      <int> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>       
## 1    0.0000000001          8 roc_auc binary     0.844     5  0.0225 pre0_mod03_…
## 2    0.0000000001         11 roc_auc binary     0.844     5  0.0225 pre0_mod04_…
## 3    0.0000000001         15 roc_auc binary     0.844     5  0.0225 pre0_mod05_…
## 4    0.0000000178          8 roc_auc binary     0.844     5  0.0225 pre0_mod08_…
## 5    0.0000000178         11 roc_auc binary     0.844     5  0.0225 pre0_mod09_…
dt_tune_rs %>% show_best(metric = "accuracy")
## # A tibble: 5 × 8
##   cost_complexity tree_depth .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config    
##             <dbl>      <int> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>      
## 1    0.0000000001          8 accuracy binary     0.888     5  0.0162 pre0_mod03…
## 2    0.0000000001         11 accuracy binary     0.888     5  0.0162 pre0_mod04…
## 3    0.0000000001         15 accuracy binary     0.888     5  0.0162 pre0_mod05…
## 4    0.0000000178          8 accuracy binary     0.888     5  0.0162 pre0_mod08…
## 5    0.0000000178         11 accuracy binary     0.888     5  0.0162 pre0_mod09…
# Choose the simplest tree within 1 std error of the best ROC AUC 
chosen_dt <- dt_tune_rs %>%
  select_by_one_std_err(metric = "roc_auc", cost_complexity, tree_depth)

final_dt <- dt_wf_tune %>%
  finalize_workflow(chosen_dt)

final_dt
## ══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## Preprocessor: Recipe
## Model: decision_tree()
## 
## ── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## 3 Recipe Steps
## 
## • step_tokenize()
## • step_tokenfilter()
## • step_tfidf()
## 
## ── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Decision Tree Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   cost_complexity = 1e-10
##   tree_depth = 8
## 
## Computational engine: rpart

Task 2 — Hyperparameter explanation

We tune cost_complexity and tree_depth. Cost_complexity penalizes higher values resulting in simpler trees that are less likely to memorize rare word patterns. Tree_depth controls how deep the tree can grow, we tune to find a balance between capturing word interactions and overfitting sparse text features.

Task 3 — Test-set evaluation and model comparison

# Refit tuned tree on all training rows
dt_test_metrics <- last_fit(final_dt, incidents_split) %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  mutate(model = "Decision tree")

dt_test_metrics
## # A tibble: 3 × 5
##   .metric     .estimator .estimate .config         model        
##   <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>           <chr>        
## 1 accuracy    binary        0.901  pre0_mod0_post0 Decision tree
## 2 roc_auc     binary        0.879  pre0_mod0_post0 Decision tree
## 3 brier_class binary        0.0822 pre0_mod0_post0 Decision tree
# Same last_fit pattern for Naive Bayes
nb_test_metrics <- last_fit(nb_wf, incidents_split) %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  mutate(model = "Naive Bayes")

nb_test_metrics
## # A tibble: 3 × 5
##   .metric     .estimator .estimate .config         model      
##   <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>           <chr>      
## 1 accuracy    binary         0.796 pre0_mod0_post0 Naive Bayes
## 2 roc_auc     binary         0.783 pre0_mod0_post0 Naive Bayes
## 3 brier_class binary         0.204 pre0_mod0_post0 Naive Bayes
# Same last_fit pattern for tuned Lasso
lasso_test_metrics <- last_fit(final_lasso, incidents_split) %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  mutate(model = "Lasso")

lasso_test_metrics
## # A tibble: 3 × 5
##   .metric     .estimator .estimate .config         model
##   <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>           <chr>
## 1 accuracy    binary        0.919  pre0_mod0_post0 Lasso
## 2 roc_auc     binary        0.951  pre0_mod0_post0 Lasso
## 3 brier_class binary        0.0620 pre0_mod0_post0 Lasso
# Side-by-side test metrics for all three classifiers
model_comparison <- bind_rows(
  nb_test_metrics,
  lasso_test_metrics,
  dt_test_metrics
) %>%
  select(model, .metric, .estimate) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = .estimate)

model_comparison
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##   model         accuracy roc_auc brier_class
##   <chr>            <dbl>   <dbl>       <dbl>
## 1 Naive Bayes      0.796   0.783      0.204 
## 2 Lasso            0.919   0.951      0.0620
## 3 Decision tree    0.901   0.879      0.0822

Task 3 — Which model performs best? Compared to the Naive Bayes and decision tree, the Lasso has the best test performance on both accuracy and ROC AUC. The decision tree is in the middle, and Naive Bayes performs worst on the test set. I think this is because the Lasso’s regularization reduces overfitting while still capturing the most important signals. As opposed to Naive Bayes which assumes feature independence and is less flexible, and the decision tree which may not capture complex interactions as well as a linear model with regularization.

Task 4 — Confusion matrix and class imbalance

# Hold-out test predictions from the tuned decision tree
dt_last_fit <- last_fit(final_dt, incidents_split)

dt_test_preds <- dt_last_fit %>%
  collect_predictions()

conf_mat(dt_test_preds, truth = deadly, estimate = .pred_class)
##             Truth
## Prediction   deadly non-deadly
##   deadly         80         22
##   non-deadly     33        420
# Plot confusion matrix heatmap
conf_mat(dt_test_preds, truth = deadly, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  labs(title = "Decision tree — test set confusion matrix")

Task 4a — What does the confusion matrix show?

Our matrix reveals that the decision tree model does reasonably on the minority class but still misses about 29% of actual deadly cases. The imbalance means that the model is much better at recognizing non-deadly language than deadly language. Which could be a result of the language used in our data, that might censor or under-describe deadly incidents, making them harder to learn from.

  • True positives: 80 deadly incidents correctly flagged as deadly.
  • False negatives: 33 deadly incidents predicted as non-deadly.
  • False positives: 22 non-deadly reports flagged as deadly.
  • True negatives: 420 non-deadly correctly classified—the majority class dominates cell counts.

The majority class (non-deadly) dominates the data, so the model is biased towards predicting that class correctly at the expense of missing deadly cases. Which is why I don’t think overall accuracy is enough to evaluate model performance in this context. Instead I would prioritize recall on the deadly class, for minimizing false negatives, even if it means more false positives, because missing a deadly report is more costly than flagging an extra non-deadly one for review because we are dealing with real human lives.
